content:
# Acts Chapter 22 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 253-254 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_022_page_001.jpg, ACT_022_page_002.jpg

---

**1** "Brothers and fathers, hear me out — let me give you my defense." **2** When they heard him speaking in the Hebrew language, they grew even quieter. Then he said:

**3** "I am a Jew, born in Tarsus of Cilicia but raised right here in this city. I sat at Gamaliel's feet and was trained with strict precision in our ancestral Law. I burned with zeal for God — exactly the way every one of you does today. **4** I hunted down the followers of this Way to the death. I had men and women alike bound in chains and thrown into prison. **5** The high priest himself can confirm this, along with the whole council of elders. I even carried letters from them to our brothers in Damascus and was on my way there to drag those people back to Jerusalem in chains for punishment.

**6** "But as I was traveling — it was about noon — and approaching Damascus, suddenly a blinding light from heaven blazed all around me. **7** I fell to the ground and heard a voice saying to me, 'Saul, Saul, why are you hunting me down?' **8** I answered, 'Who are you, Lord?' And he said to me, 'I am Jesus the Nazarene — the one you are hunting down.' **9** The men with me saw the light, but they could not make out the voice speaking to me. **10** I said, 'What should I do, Lord?' And the Lord told me, 'Get up. Go into Damascus. There you will be told everything that has been laid out for you to do.' **11** Since I could not see — the brilliance of that light had blinded me — my companions took me by the hand and led me into Damascus.

**12** "A man named Ananias came to me. He was devout, faithful to the Law, well regarded by every Jew living there. **13** He stood beside me and said, 'Brother Saul, receive your sight!' And at that very moment I looked up and could see him. **14** Then he said, 'The God of our ancestors has handpicked you to know his will, to see the Righteous One face to face, and to hear the words from his own mouth. **15** Because you will be his witness to all people, telling them what you have seen and heard. **16** So what are you waiting for? Get up! Be baptized and have your sins washed away, calling on his name.'

**17** "Later, after I had returned to Jerusalem and was praying in the temple, I fell into a trance. **18** I saw him — and he said to me, 'Hurry! Get out of Jerusalem at once, because they will not accept your testimony about me.' **19** I said, 'Lord, they know perfectly well that I went from one synagogue to the next, imprisoning and beating those who believed in you. **20** And when the blood of your witness Stephen was being poured out, I was standing right there — giving my full approval and guarding the cloaks of those who killed him.' **21** But he said to me, 'Go. I am sending you far away — to the Gentiles.'"

**22** The crowd listened to him up to that word. Then they erupted: "Wipe this man off the face of the earth! He does not deserve to live!" **23** They were screaming, ripping their cloaks off, and hurling dust into the air. **24** So the commanding officer ordered Paul brought into the barracks. He directed that he be examined under flogging, to find out why the crowd was raging against him like this.

**25** But as they stretched him out for the lash, Paul said to the centurion standing there, "Is it legal for you to flog a Roman citizen who hasn't even been tried?" **26** When the centurion heard this, he went straight to the commanding officer and reported it. "Watch what you're doing," he said. "This man is a Roman citizen." **27** The commanding officer came over and asked Paul directly, "Tell me — are you a Roman citizen?" "Yes," he said. **28** The officer replied, "I paid an enormous sum for my citizenship." Paul said, "But I was born one." **29** Immediately those who were about to interrogate him backed away. And the commanding officer himself was alarmed when he realized he had put a Roman citizen in chains.

**30** The next day, determined to get to the bottom of what the Jews were actually charging Paul with, he released him from his bonds. He ordered the chief priests and the entire council to assemble, then brought Paul down and stood him before them.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

This chapter is one of the great courtroom scenes of the New Testament — Paul standing on the fortress steps, speaking in Hebrew to a mob that moments ago was trying to kill him. The speech is a masterpiece of rhetorical strategy, and the dynamic rendering aims to capture that urgency.

**Verse 2 — "Hebrew language" (te Hebraidi dialekto):** Sinaiticus reads this as the Hebrew/Aramaic dialect. The crowd's reaction — growing quieter — is dramatically significant. Paul is signaling that he is one of them. I rendered the effect ("grew even quieter") to emphasize the hush falling over the volatile crowd.

**Verse 3 — "I burned with zeal for God":** The Greek zelotes huparchon tou theou carries more than intellectual commitment. "Zealot" in this context means fanatical devotion. "Burned with zeal" captures the visceral intensity Paul is deliberately invoking — he is mirroring back to the crowd their own fury.

**Verse 4 — "hunted down... to the death" (edioksa... achri thanatou):** The verb dioko means to pursue, persecute, hunt. "Hunted down" conveys the predatory energy Paul is confessing to. "This Way" (ten hodon) is the early self-designation of the Jesus movement.

**Verse 6 — "blinding light blazed" (periestrapsen phos):** The verb periastrapto means to flash around, to engulf in light. "Blazed" conveys the sudden, overwhelming force of the theophany. Sinaiticus reads phos hikanon, a "considerable/sufficient light" — meaning not faint but overpowering.

**Verse 14 — "handpicked you" (proecheirisato se):** This rare verb (procheirizomai) means to appoint, designate, or choose beforehand. "Handpicked" captures the personal, deliberate selection Paul is describing — God's direct agency.

**Verse 16 — "What are you waiting for?":** The Greek ti melleis is literally "why do you delay?" The dynamic rendering sharpens Ananias's urgency. The washing of sins (apolousal tas hamartias sou) tied to baptism is theologically significant — Ananias presents baptism as the enacted moment of cleansing.

**Verse 20 — "your witness Stephen" (Stephanou tou marturos sou):** Sinaiticus uses martus here — the word that would become "martyr." Stephen is called God's "witness," but the blood-language (haima... exechunneto) makes the martyrological meaning unmistakable. I kept "witness" but added "blood... being poured out" to carry the weight.

**Verse 22 — The eruption:** The Greek airen apo tes ges ton toiouton ("take away from the earth such a person") becomes "Wipe this man off the face of the earth" — capturing the murderous fury. The word "Gentiles" (ethne) is the trigger. Everything Paul said was acceptable until he claimed God sent him away from Israel to the nations.

**Verse 25 — The citizenship bombshell:** Paul's question (ei anthropon Romaion kai akatakriton exestin humin mastizein) is a legal masterstroke. "Who hasn't even been tried" (akatakriton) is the key word — it is doubly illegal to flog a Roman citizen, and triply so without a trial. The entire power dynamic reverses in a single sentence.

**Verse 28 — "I was born one":** The contrast between purchased and birthright citizenship (ego de kai gegennemai) is devastating in its brevity. The officer's expensive acquisition is trumped by Paul's inherited status. The Greek is terse; the English should be equally sharp.

-- PNEUMA
